meta.name: Excited Delirium Progressing to Sudden Cardiac Arrest

patient.name: Marcus Riggins
patient.chiefComplaint: Severe agitation, naked in street, suspected stimulant intoxication

history.hpi: 34-year-old male found running in the street with no clothes on a 95°F afternoon, screaming incoherently, sweating profusely. Witnesses report he was "on something" and had been acting strangely for several hours. Police arrived first and have him in handcuffs prone on the asphalt; they describe a prolonged struggle to control him. No reported trauma. Patient is hot to touch, diaphoretic, with dilated pupils and bizarre, paranoid speech.
pastMedical: Unknown — no reliable history available on scene
medications: Unknown — patient unable to provide reliable history
allergies: NKDA per bystanders / police database check
history.lastOralIntake: Unknown — witnesses last saw him eating roughly 6 hours ago
history.events: Witnesses observed patient running from a residence shouting that people were after him. He stripped off his clothes in the street, paced erratically, and assaulted a bystander before police arrived. Officers report he was extraordinarily strong, did not respond to verbal commands, and continued struggling after being handcuffed.

scene.dispatch: Engine and medic, respond Code 3 for a violent subject, 34-year-old male, altered and combative, PD on scene at 4400 block of West National Avenue.
scene.location: Open residential street, mid-afternoon. Patient prone on hot asphalt, handcuffed behind back by two officers. Outside temperature 95°F with bright sun.
scene.safety: Scene secured by two on-scene officers. Patient remains combative despite restraint. Continued prone positioning with weight on torso is a positional asphyxia risk — must be addressed immediately on EMS arrival.
scene.bystanders: Multiple witnesses on the sidewalk; one reports patient "was smoking something" and "thinks the devil is after him." No family or known contacts on scene.
visualCues: Patient naked except for boxers, lying prone on asphalt
Profuse diaphoresis visible despite hot, dry environment
Drug paraphernalia (small glass pipe) on lawn nearby
Officers visibly fatigued, one kneeling across patient's upper back
Patient screaming nonsensical phrases, biting at the ground

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: Arrival on scene. Patient prone on hot asphalt with two officers maintaining handcuff restraint. Sympathomimetic toxidrome is unmistakable: severely agitated, diaphoretic, hyperthermic, tachycardic, hypertensive, with mydriasis. PD reports an extended struggle. This is a textbook excited delirium / delirium with agitated behavior scenario with extreme risk of sudden cardiopulmonary arrest. Crew must establish scene safety, get the patient off the prone position, complete a rapid focused assessment, and prepare for both chemical restraint and aggressive cooling.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent but at risk — patient screaming, biting at ground, prone with officer weight on upper torso. Positional asphyxia concern.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Respirations rapid and deep at 32/min, accessory muscle use, no stridor or wheeze. Lungs clear bilaterally.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Radial pulse rapid and bounding at 162. Skin flushed, soaking wet with sweat, hot to touch. Capillary refill <2 sec.
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Extreme agitation. Thrashing all four extremities against handcuffs and officers. No focal deficit appreciable.
otherFindings: Tactile temperature markedly elevated; oral/temporal scan reads 104.0°F
No obvious head, chest, or abdominal trauma despite prolonged struggle
Bystander reports stimulant use; glass pipe observed nearby
Patient yelling paranoid, disorganized speech; not following commands
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: "Get off me! They're coming for me! Don't let them in!" — disorganized, paranoid, no orientation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Confirm PD scene control; do not enter until secure; coordinate roles
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Don PPE. Do not attempt to enter or control a scene where physical violence or weapons are present. Dispatch law enforcement immediately to secure and maintain scene safety.
expectedActions.rationale: Tier-3 behavioral arrests start with scene control. EMS cannot manage this patient without continuous LE support.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.shortAction: Get patient OFF prone position immediately; never hog-tie or weight on chest
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The following techniques should be expressly prohibited for use by EMS clinicians: Secure or transport in a prone position with or without hands and feet behind the back (hobbling or 'hog-tying'); 'Sandwiching' patients between backboards; Techniques that constrict the neck or compromise the airway.
expectedActions.rationale: Prone restraint with officer weight on torso is a leading cause of sudden death in excited delirium via positional asphyxia.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.shortAction: Rapid ABCs; check glucose and temp; rule out head injury / hypoglycemia
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Exclusion Criteria: Patients exhibiting agitated or violent behavior due to medical conditions including, but not limited to: Head injury; Metabolic disorders (e.g., hypoglycemia, hypoxia). Assessment: Maintain and support airway; Note respiratory rate and effort – If possible, monitor pulse oximetry and/or capnography; Assess mental status — Check blood glucose (if possible); Obtain temperature (if possible); Assess for evidence of traumatic injuries.
expectedActions.rationale: Hypoglycemia and head injury mimic excited delirium and must be excluded before assuming a toxic-behavioral etiology.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize delirium-with-agitated-behavior — imminent arrest risk; verbalize to crew
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Uncontrolled or poorly controlled patient agitation and physical violence can place the patient at risk for sudden cardiopulmonary arrest due to the following etiologies: Delirium with agitated behavior: A postmortem diagnosis of exclusion for sudden death thought to result from metabolic acidosis (most likely from lactate) stemming from physical agitation or physical control measures and potentially exacerbated by stimulant drugs (e.g., cocaine) or alcohol withdrawal; Positional asphyxia: Sudden death from restriction of chest wall movement and/or obstruction of the airway secondary to restricted head or neck positioning resulting in hypercarbia and/or hypoxia.
expectedActions.rationale: Naming the syndrome aloud forces the team to pre-plan for arrest, set up the monitor early, and avoid the false security of "patient just needs to be sedated."
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.shortAction: Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, and capnography NOW — before sedation
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Apply a cardiac monitor as soon as possible, particularly when pharmacologic management medications have been administered. All patients who have received pharmacologic management medications must be monitored closely for the development of hypoventilation and oversedation — Utilize capnography if available.
expectedActions.rationale: Monitor must be on the patient BEFORE chemical restraint so the inevitable PEA or wide-complex deterioration is witnessed and time-stamped.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

transitions.condition: Crew has flipped patient supine, established PD-supported control, applied monitor and capnography, ruled out hypoglycemia (glucose 138) and head trauma, and proceeded to chemical restraint (ketamine 4 mg/kg IM OR midazolam 5 mg IM) with simultaneous active cooling (ice packs to neck/axillae/groin, clothing removed, moved to shaded/AC environment).

Crew administers chemical restraint and then directs attention to paperwork, packaging, or moving equipment without applying the cardiac monitor, SpO2, and capnography to the patient. Patient is left under PD watch without continuous EMS monitoring.

Crew sedates patient and loads to a warm ambulance compartment without removing clothing, without applying ice packs, and without lowering compartment temperature. Active cooling is deferred until after arrival at hospital.

phases.name: Chemical restraint + cooling
phases.description: Patient repositioned supine with soft restraint to stretcher, all four extremities individually secured, no chest-wall restriction. Ketamine 4 mg/kg IM (or midazolam 5 mg IM) administered for chemical restraint; clothing removed; ice packs placed to neck, axillae, and groin; ambulance compartment pre-cooled. Cardiac monitor, SpO2, and capnography in place. Over the next 3–5 minutes the sympathomimetic surge starts to wane: heart rate and BP drift down, patient sedates to GCS 11, but he is still hyperthermic and acidotic from the prolonged struggle. The team must NOT relax — this is exactly when delirium-with-agitated-behavior patients arrest.
phases.triggerCondition: Following supine repositioning, chemical restraint with ketamine 4 mg/kg IM (90 kg × 4 mg/kg = 360 mg) OR midazolam 5 mg IM, simultaneous active cooling, and continuous monitoring; patient transitions from extreme agitation to sedated state over 3–5 minutes.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent. Snoring respirations resolved with simple jaw thrust / NPA. No vomiting.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Respirations 22/min, regular, deep. Lungs clear. SpO2 holding.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Radial pulse strong, regular. Skin still hot to touch, diaphoretic but less so. Cap refill <2 sec.
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Sedated. Minimal spontaneous movement, withdraws to pain. Restraints loose on extremities, no struggle.
otherFindings: Tactile temperature still elevated; tympanic 103.6°F
IV established 18g R AC; 1 L NS cool, running wide open
Ice packs in place: neck, both axillae, both groins
Patient packaged supine on stretcher, soft restraints x4 to stretcher frame
EtCO2 waveform present, value trending up from 28 → 32
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Mumbling, no longer paranoid; intermittent groans.

expectedActions.shortAction: Ketamine 360 mg IM (90 kg × 4 mg/kg = 360 mg); 3–5 min onset
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Dissociative Agents (provide sedation and anesthesia) — Ketamine (option for high violence risk): Adults: 2 mg/kg IV; 1 minute onset of action OR 4 mg/kg IM; 3–5 minute onset of action.
expectedActions.rationale: Ketamine is the agent of choice for delirium with agitated behavior — fast IM onset, preserves airway/respiratory drive, breaks the catecholamine surge.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.shortAction: Alternative: Midazolam 5 mg IM (10–15 min onset)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Benzodiazepines — Midazolam: Adults: 5 mg IV; 3–5 minute onset of action OR 5 mg IM; 10–15 minute onset of action OR 5 mg IN; 3–5 minute onset of action.
expectedActions.rationale: If ketamine unavailable or contraindicated, midazolam IM is the protocol alternative. Slower onset — plan accordingly.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.shortAction: Supine; soft restraints x4 to stretcher frame; never restrict chest wall
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Physical Management Devices — Body: Stretcher straps should be applied as the standard procedure for all patients during transport; Physical management devices, including stretcher straps, should never restrict the patient's chest wall motion. Extremities: Secure all four extremities to maximize safety for patient, staff, and others; Secure all extremities to the stationary frame of the stretcher.
expectedActions.rationale: Supine, four-point, no chest restriction, no prone — directly mitigates positional asphyxia risk.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.shortAction: Active cooling: strip clothes, ice packs to neck/axillae/groin, cool NS bolus
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If core temperature is greater than 104°F (40°C) or if altered mental status is present, begin active cooling by: Ice bath immersion provides the most rapid cooling mechanism; If ice bath immersion is not available, consider the following: Tarp-assisted cooling with oscillation; Rotating ice water-soaked towels or sheets; Continually misting the exposed skin with tepid water while fanning the victim; Truncal ice packs may be used, but are less effective than evaporation. Establish IV access for patients suffering from heat stroke — give cool fluids at 20 mL/kg boluses and reduce to 10 mL/kg/hr boluses when vitals are stable.
expectedActions.rationale: Hyperthermia is the dominant kill mechanism; sedation alone without cooling does not save these patients.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Hyperthermia/Heat Exposure

expectedActions.shortAction: IV access; 1 L cool NS bolus (90 kg × 20 mL/kg = 1800 mL; start with 1 L)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Give fluids for poor perfusion; cool fluids for hyperthermia. Establish IV access for patients suffering from heat stroke — give cool fluids at 20 mL/kg boluses and reduce to 10 mL/kg/hr boluses when vitals are stable.
expectedActions.rationale: Cool IV fluids address volume losses from prolonged exertion and contribute to cooling.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Stimulant Poisoning/Overdose

expectedActions.shortAction: Continuous ECG, SpO2, EtCO2 — watch for hypoventilation, QRS widening, arrhythmia
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: All patients who have received pharmacologic management medications must be monitored closely for the development of hypoventilation and oversedation — Utilize capnography if available. Pertinent Assessment Findings: Continuous monitoring of: Airway patency; Respiratory status with pulse oximetry and/or capnography; Circulatory status with frequent blood pressure measurements; Cardiac status, especially if the patient has received pharmacologic management medication.
expectedActions.rationale: This is the window where the patient quietly decompensates into PEA. Eyes on monitor, not on paperwork.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.shortAction: 12-lead EKG; watch for wide QRS (cocaine → Na-channel block)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Notes/Educational Pearls — Cocaine has sodium channel blocking effects and can cause significant cardiac conduction abnormalities with a widened QRS. Treatment is with sodium bicarbonate similar to a tricyclic antidepressant. Check a 12-lead EKG to assess for these complications.
expectedActions.rationale: A wide QRS pre-arrest tells you sodium bicarb will be needed if/when the patient codes.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Stimulant Poisoning/Overdose

transitions.condition: Despite appropriate management, patient abruptly loses pulse. Monitor shows narrow-complex electrical activity that briefly widens, then progresses to PEA. EtCO2 drops, SpO2 plummets. This is the classic delirium-with-agitated-behavior sudden death event — typically 5–10 minutes after the catecholamine surge ends.

phases.name: Sudden PEA arrest
phases.description: Patient abruptly loses pulse on the stretcher. Monitor shows organized but widening QRS at 70, progressing to PEA. EtCO2 drops from 32 to 12. This is the moment. The entire scenario pivots: the team must clear restraints, expose the chest, initiate compressions, and run an ACLS algorithm tailored to this patient's reversible causes — hyperthermia (continue cooling), acidosis from prolonged struggle (sodium bicarb), and sodium-channel toxicity if QRS wide (sodium bicarb). Epinephrine 1 mg IV early (PEA = non-shockable, prioritize epi). NO defibrillation indication unless rhythm changes.
phases.triggerCondition: Continuous monitor shows abrupt deterioration: HR drops from 132 → 70 with widening QRS, then loss of pulse with organized PEA waveform; EtCO2 collapses from 32 → 12; SpO2 unobtainable. Witnessed arrest in the back of the ambulance / on scene, monitor in place.

clinicalPresentation.airway: No spontaneous respiratory effort. BVM with OPA in place.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Apneic. Ventilating at 10/min via BVM with high-flow O2.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Pulseless. CPR in progress, two-handed compressions at 100–120/min on stretcher locked into ambulance.
otherFindings: Restraints CLEARED from torso and arm with IV; left wrist soft-restrained for line protection
Backboard slid under torso for compression surface
Ice packs still in place; ambient cooling continuing
IV patent; pads applied
Code timer started

expectedActions.shortAction: Clear restraints from chest/arms; high-quality CPR; pads on
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Initiate chest compressions in cases with no bystander chest compressions or take over compressions from bystanders while a second rescuer is setting up the AED or defibrillator. Key Considerations: Effective chest compressions are defined as: A rate of greater than 100 and less than 120 compressions/minute; Depth of at least 2 inches (5 cm) and less than 2.4 inches (6 cm) for adults; Allow for complete chest recoil; Minimize interruptions in compressions.
expectedActions.rationale: Restraints are the single thing that distinguishes this arrest from any other PEA — you cannot compress effectively through soft restraints across the chest.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA)

expectedActions.shortAction: Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO (1:10,000) q3–5 min; PRIORITIZE early in PEA
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: If feasible, IV or IO access should be obtained. Administer Epinephrine 1 mg during the first or second round of compressions. Prioritize early administration of epinephrine for non-shockable rhythms.
expectedActions.rationale: PEA is non-shockable; protocol explicitly prioritizes early epinephrine.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA)

expectedActions.shortAction: Sodium bicarbonate 90 mEq IV (90 kg × 1 mEq/kg = 90 mEq) for acidosis / wide QRS
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider reversible causes of cardiac arrest which include the following — The dialysis patient/known hyperkalemic patient: Sodium bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg IV. Tricyclic antidepressant overdose. Additions to care include sodium bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg IV. Stimulant: Cocaine has sodium channel blocking effects and can cause significant cardiac conduction abnormalities with a widened QRS. Treatment is with sodium bicarbonate similar to a tricyclic antidepressant.
expectedActions.rationale: Two coupled indications in this patient — profound lactic acidosis from prolonged struggle, plus possible cocaine-induced sodium-channel blockade with wide QRS.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA)

expectedActions.shortAction: CONTINUE active cooling during arrest — ice packs, exposed chest, cool NS
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Cooling efforts should continue until the patient's temperature is less than 102.2°F (39°C) or, if continuous temperature monitoring is not available, until the patient demonstrates improvement in mental status. For patients with signs and symptoms of heat stroke, rapid cooling takes priority over other interventions (e.g., cardiac monitoring, IV access).
expectedActions.rationale: Hyperthermia is a reversible cause and will keep killing the patient even after ROSC if untreated.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Hyperthermia/Heat Exposure

expectedActions.shortAction: Cool NS 2 L wide-open (Hs: hypovolemia from prolonged exertion/diaphoresis)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider reversible causes of cardiac arrest which include the following — Hypovolemia. Additions to care include normal saline 2 L IV (or 20 mL/kg, repeated up to 3 times for pediatrics).
expectedActions.rationale: These patients are profoundly volume-depleted from sustained exertion, mydriatic sweating, and hyperthermia.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA)

expectedActions.shortAction: Use EtCO2 to gauge compression quality (target >10–20 mmHg; rising = good)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Quantitative end-tidal capnography (EtCO2) should be used to monitor effectiveness of chest compressions — If EtCO2 less than 10 mmHg during the initial phases of resuscitation, attempt to improve chest compression quality.
expectedActions.rationale: EtCO2 12 at compression onset signals poor circulation — push harder, push deeper, and a rising EtCO2 is the first sign you'll see before ROSC.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA)

expectedActions.shortAction: BVM 10/min with OPA; advanced airway deferred until 3–4 cycles done
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: BVM ventilation at 10 breaths per minute (1 breath every 10 compressions), applied during the upstroke between compressions, without interrupting the compressions. During the first four cycles of compressions/defibrillation (approximately 10 minutes) avoid advanced airway placement.
expectedActions.rationale: Compressions and epi/bicarb first; advanced airway is a distractor in the first 10 minutes.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA)

expectedActions.shortAction: DO NOT defibrillate — PEA is non-shockable; reassess rhythm q2 min
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The most important therapies for patients suffering from cardiac arrest are prompt cardiac defibrillation for shockable rhythms and minimally interrupted effective chest compressions. Continue the cycle of chest compressions for 2 minutes, followed by rhythm analysis and defibrillation of shockable rhythms.
expectedActions.rationale: Reflex defibrillation of a non-shockable rhythm wastes time and energy and pauses compressions.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA)

transitions.condition: After 2–3 rounds of CPR + epi + sodium bicarb, with active cooling continued and 2 L cool NS in, palpable pulse returns. Rhythm reorganizes to sinus tachycardia. BP weak, GCS deeply depressed, EtCO2 rises, but patient is hyperthermic, acidotic, and at very high risk of re-arrest.

phases.name: Fragile ROSC
phases.description: Pulse returns at 88 sinus tach. BP 86/52, EtCO2 climbing to 28, but patient remains deeply comatose (GCS 4), core temp still 102.4°F, and the team is at maximum risk of complacency. Re-arrest is common in this exact window. Goals: secure airway, avoid hyperventilation, maintain SpO2 94–98%, support BP, KEEP COOLING, serial 12-leads (rule out STEMI / wide QRS), transport to closest appropriate facility with cath/critical-care capability. Mechanical CPR or hands-ready posture maintained against re-arrest.
phases.triggerCondition: Following appropriate ACLS — CPR, epinephrine, sodium bicarbonate, continued cooling, and 2 L cool NS — ROSC is achieved approximately 6–8 minutes into the arrest. Patient is profoundly post-arrest: hypotensive, comatose, still hyperthermic, with high re-arrest risk.

clinicalPresentation.airway: No protective reflexes. BVM with OPA, smooth ventilation. Plan for advanced airway en route.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Occasional agonal effort; ventilating at 10/min, easy compliance.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Weak central pulse, palpable carotid and femoral. Cap refill 4 sec. Skin still hot.
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Flexion to pain, no spontaneous movement.
otherFindings: ROSC time logged
Ice packs in place; ambulance compartment 65°F
Cool NS 1.5 L in; second line considered
12-lead obtained: sinus rhythm, no STEMI, QRS now 100 ms (was 130 ms intra-arrest)
Pads remain on patient; defibrillator armed and ready

expectedActions.shortAction: BVM 8–10/min; target EtCO2 35–45; do NOT hyperventilate
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Do not hyperventilate. Maintain a ventilation rate of 8–10 breaths per minute, targeting an EtCO2 of 35–45 mmHg. Hyperventilation is a significant cause of hypotension and recurrence of cardiac arrest in the post resuscitation phase and must be avoided.
expectedActions.rationale: Most common cause of post-ROSC re-arrest is hyperventilation. Slow, controlled BVM rate.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

expectedActions.shortAction: Titrate FiO2 to SpO2 94–98% — do not hyperoxygenate
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation. Do not hyperoxygenate.
expectedActions.rationale: Hyperoxia post-ROSC worsens neuro outcomes.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

expectedActions.shortAction: Continue active cooling until core temp <102.2°F — patient is 102.4°F
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Cooling efforts should continue until the patient's temperature is less than 102.2°F (39°C) or, if continuous temperature monitoring is not available, until the patient demonstrates improvement in mental status.
expectedActions.rationale: Hyperthermia post-ROSC = guaranteed re-arrest if untreated. Cooling does not stop at ROSC.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Hyperthermia/Heat Exposure

expectedActions.shortAction: Serial 12-lead EKG — assess for STEMI, persistent wide QRS
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Perform serial 12-lead EKGs to assess for evidence of reversible cause of arrest such as STEMI or electrolyte derangement (e.g., hyperkalemia). Post-cardiac arrest patients with evidence or interpretation consistent with ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI/acute MI) should be transported preferably to a facility capable of emergent cardiac catheterization.
expectedActions.rationale: Stimulant-induced vasospasm can produce STEMI; persistent wide QRS argues for more sodium bicarb.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

expectedActions.shortAction: Continue cool NS bolus; reassess BP q3 min; prep push-dose epi if SBP <90
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For hypotension (SBP less than 90 mmHg or MAP less than 65 in adults) see Shock Guideline. Many patients experience 'stunning' of the cardiac muscle after ROSC. Hypotension is common, and volume resuscitation or vasopressor support is often required.
expectedActions.rationale: SBP 86 is below the 90 threshold; fluid first, vasopressor on standby.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

expectedActions.shortAction: Anticipate re-arrest; pads on, defibrillator armed, mechanical CPR ready
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Monitor closely for recurrence of cardiac arrest using clinical and adjunctive criteria such as cardiac monitoring, EtCO2 monitoring, and physical signs of perfusion. The condition of post-resuscitation patients fluctuates rapidly and continuously requiring close monitoring. A significant percentage of post-ROSC patients will re-arrest. Maintain mechanical CPR device in place in preparation for re-arrest.
expectedActions.rationale: Excited delirium ROSC is one of the most fragile post-arrest states in EMS.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport to closest appropriate center (cath-capable / post-arrest care)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider transporting patients to an age-appropriate facility which offers specialized adult or pediatric post-resuscitation care. Post-cardiac arrest patients with evidence or interpretation consistent with ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI/acute MI) should be transported preferably to a facility capable of emergent cardiac catheterization or, as a secondary option, to a STEMI receiving facility.
expectedActions.rationale: Specialized post-arrest care meaningfully improves neuro outcomes.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

phases.name: No monitor after sedation
phases.description: Crew administered ketamine 4 mg/kg IM and then turned to paperwork and equipment, leaving the patient under PD watch on the lawn without the cardiac monitor, SpO2, or capnography in place. Approximately 4 minutes later, an officer notices the patient is no longer breathing and is unresponsive — the arrest is unwitnessed by EMS. By the time monitor is finally applied, rhythm is fine VF transitioning to asystole, downtime is uncertain, and EtCO2 is <10. Outcome is poor; resuscitation should still be attempted but the lost monitoring window cost the patient.
phases.triggerCondition: Chemical restraint was administered without simultaneous application of the cardiac monitor, SpO2, and capnography. Patient was left under PD supervision while crew attended to scene/equipment. Arrest was unwitnessed by EMS, with unknown downtime before recognition.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Apneic; cyanosis around lips. No protective reflexes.
otherFindings: Arrest was UNWITNESSED — downtime estimated 3–5 minutes before recognition
First monitored rhythm: fine VF deteriorating to asystole
EtCO2 reads <10 mmHg — perfusion poor, prognosis grim
Restraints still in place across torso when arrest discovered — delayed compressions further
Crew now scrambling to set up monitor and IV that should have been ready

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize lesson: monitor BEFORE / WITH sedation, never after
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Apply a cardiac monitor as soon as possible, particularly when pharmacologic management medications have been administered. All patients who have received pharmacologic management medications must be monitored closely for the development of hypoventilation and oversedation.
expectedActions.rationale: The protocol is explicit: monitor accompanies sedation. This is the single highest-yield teaching point of the scenario.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.shortAction: Initiate late CPR; defib VF; epi q3–5 min; outcome likely poor
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Defibrillation dosing should follow manufacturer's recommendation in the case of biphasic defibrillators. Zoll — Adult – 120 J, 150 J, 200 J. Administer Epinephrine 1 mg during the first or second round of compressions.
expectedActions.rationale: Once the arrest is recognized, ACLS proceeds normally but the unwitnessed downtime drastically reduces survival.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA)

phases.name: Hot transport, no cooling
phases.description: Patient was sedated with ketamine 4 mg/kg IM and loaded into a warm ambulance compartment still wearing his clothing, with no ice packs and no compartment cooling. Crew is monitoring (ECG/SpO2/EtCO2) but has deferred active cooling. En route, core temperature climbs from 104.0°F to 105.4°F; rhythm degrades to wide-complex sinus tach with PVCs, then to wide-complex PEA. Resuscitation is started but the persistent hyperthermia + acidosis make ROSC unlikely without aggressive in-the-back cooling that should have been initiated on scene.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew chemically restrained patient and transported without removing clothing, applying ice packs, or pre-cooling the patient compartment. Hyperthermia worsened en route, driving the patient into wide-complex deterioration and arrest.

otherFindings: Cessation of sweating noted en route — sign of thermoregulatory failure
Patient still in jeans and shoes; clothing not removed for cooling
Compartment temp ~78°F; AC was not maximized
First arrest rhythm: wide-complex (QRS 160 ms) sinus tach with PVCs → wide PEA
Now belatedly stripping patient and applying ice packs intra-arrest — but the window is mostly closed

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize lesson: active cooling starts ON SCENE, not after arrest
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For patients with signs and symptoms of heat stroke, rapid cooling takes priority over other interventions (e.g., cardiac monitoring, IV access). Recognition and treatment of hyperthermia (including sedatives to decrease heat production from muscular activity) is essential as many deaths are attributable to hyperthermia.
expectedActions.rationale: Hyperthermia is the dominant killer in this syndrome. Sedating without cooling guarantees decompensation.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Hyperthermia/Heat Exposure

expectedActions.shortAction: Sodium bicarb 90 mEq IV (1 mEq/kg) for wide-QRS + lactic acidosis
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Cocaine has sodium channel blocking effects and can cause significant cardiac conduction abnormalities with a widened QRS. Treatment is with sodium bicarbonate similar to a tricyclic antidepressant. Delirium with agitated behavior: A postmortem diagnosis of exclusion for sudden death thought to result from metabolic acidosis (most likely from lactate) stemming from physical agitation or physical control measures and potentially exacerbated by stimulant drugs (e.g., cocaine) or alcohol withdrawal.
expectedActions.rationale: Wide QRS plus profound lactic acidosis is the dual indication. Without bicarb, ROSC is unlikely.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Stimulant Poisoning/Overdose

expectedActions.shortAction: Strip patient now; ice packs; cool NS 2 L wide-open; max AC
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Move victim to a cool area and shield from the sun or any external heat source. Remove as much clothing as is practical and loosen any restrictive garments. Ice bath immersion provides the most rapid cooling mechanism. Truncal ice packs may be used, but are less effective than evaporation.
expectedActions.rationale: Even late, cooling is the only chance. Strip, pack, soak, and crank the AC.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Hyperthermia/Heat Exposure
